item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't figure out exactly why, but this is one of the most beautiful articles I've ever read on the site. Thank you, man, you made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took it to be EL's fiance, and gather that she's a little less pleased with whatever he managed to do than he is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, someone mentioned the 'random spooky moment' as a negative thing, so you're not the only one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't get the second apparition place in the story, but nobody else complained about that so maybe I'm just stupid. The rest of the article is pretty good, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: Why is there a hotdog in that chamber?

2: it is semi-existend.

1: semi-… How comes that?

2: Magnets and lifeforce. Its complicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a little process called "Tangential Learning". I redacted the name specifically so people would try and find it out, and educate themselves about the subject presented in this article. You've just proved that it works. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this one was pretty cool. One comment, though: why bother redacting his name, when I was able to search for 'Latvian sculptor 1887' and find it out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Polar Opposites, Magnetic Curse, the Hotdog Machine… all titles I think would be more fitting, if only because the title makes this article seem more of a joke then it is. You've got my upvote though, loved the transcript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's awful, but I'm horrible at titles. Perhaps you have a suggestion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I cared that much for the random moment of spookiness in the interview, which seems kind of jarring (and not in a good way) when considered against the rest of the article.

Other than that, though, I liked the level of detail with which this was written, along with a proper dosage of weirdness that held my interest and overcame the tiredness of the "science that works when it shouldn't" thing that we see around here from time to time. Upvoted.

EDIT: Ugh. That title is awful. Please consider changing it to something less stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added 'Florida' for the sake of professionalism. Also, it is rather round about, now that you mentioned it… might fix it later if more people object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this is the Coral Castle guy? Hell yes! Even better than Nikola Tesla. :D

I like the addition of the corpses to the milieu, I like the interview (I kind of figured that they would have each gone to one pole, but hearing it didn't decrease the impact), and I really like that the doctor brought back the hot dog.

Two things. First, I'd specify "Miami-Dade County, Florida", for professionality. Second, how the hell did the doctor understand the Latvian enough for them to transcribe it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternate sciences are interesting if they are well written like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always loved eccentric weirdos, and this captures his spirit very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had heard of Coral Castle, and I like what you've done with the article. Good job!

item.title: Author Commentary!
item.content:encoded:
This is based on the life and work of an actual person who today is considered a brilliant engineer, but also a crazy-ass psuedoscientist. It was so weird, and his work was so odd, that I figured the guy deserved a decent SCP article, because let's face it, how many people who don't live near Homestead, Florida have heard of this guy?

Thanks to everyone on chat who's read this, and I hope you enjoy!
